verses.text: The queen of Sheba comes to test Solomon with hard questions, and seeing his wisdom, his house, and his ordered court, there was 'no more spirit in her.' Report fell short of reality. The glory of God's gifts, fully seen, leaves the proud beholder breathless.

She confesses the half was not told her, and blesses the LORD who set Solomon on the throne to do justice. A foreign queen praises Israel's God. The wisdom God gives His servants becomes a witness that turns outsiders to honour Him.

She gives gold and spices in abundance, and Solomon grants her every desire in return. Royal generosity meets royal generosity. The blessings God pours out are meant to overflow in open-handed giving, not to be hoarded.

Gold pours in yearly, and Solomon makes great shields of beaten gold for his house. Splendour fills the kingdom. Yet these golden shields, soon carried off by Egypt, warn that earthly glory is borrowed and quickly fades.

Solomon makes a great ivory throne overlaid with gold, with lions on its steps, unmatched in any kingdom. His seat of judgment is glorious. It dimly pictures a greater throne — the King whose righteous reign no kingdom can rival.

All his vessels are gold; silver is counted as nothing in Solomon's days, his ships returning laden every three years. Riches abound beyond reckoning. When God prospers, He can make the precious common — yet such abundance is a trust, not a trophy.

All his vessels are gold; silver is counted as nothing in Solomon's days, his ships returning laden every three years. Riches abound beyond reckoning. When God prospers, He can make the precious common — yet such abundance is a trust, not a trophy.

Solomon surpasses all kings of the earth in riches and wisdom, and all the world seeks his presence, bringing gifts year by year. His fame draws the nations. The wisdom that comes from God lifts a man above all that human striving can reach.

He gathers stalls of horses, chariots, and horsemen, silver as plentiful as stones, importing horses out of Egypt. The wealth is staggering — yet the turn to Egypt and multiplied horses quietly breaks the king's law, sowing seeds the heart will later reap.

The rest of Solomon's acts are written by the prophets; he reigns forty years, sleeps with his fathers, and Rehoboam succeeds him. The golden reign closes. Earthly glory, however great, ends in a grave; only God's kingdom does not pass away.
